story.title: Dr. Al-Rashid's Virus-Busting Surfaces
story.text:
Hi, I’m Dr. Fatima Al-Rashid, and I design smart surfaces that fight germs all by themselves. At the Johns Hopkins Applied Physics Lab, I’m an Antimicrobial Surfaces Engineer. It’s a big title, but my job is simple: I make materials for places like hospitals and schools that can destroy a dangerous pathogen—a germ that causes disease—on contact.

The science I use is the same science that makes washing your hands with soap and water so powerful. In my computer models, I test how different factors change the outcome. For example, I adjust the Soap Concentration, which is how much of my active ingredient is on the surface. I also have to account for if Water is Present, because just like regular soap, my coatings often need a little moisture to get activated and spread out.

When both of these factors are high, my model shows a big increase in Soap Effectiveness. This is where the magic happens. Many viruses are protected by a fatty outer layer called a lipid membrane. My coatings, and the soap you use, contain special molecules that are amphiphilic. This means one end of the molecule loves water, and the other end loves fat. The fat-loving end drills into the virus's lipid shield, breaking it apart and destroying the virus completely.

My goal is always to see the Virus Survival Rate drop to nearly zero. The more effective my surface is, the fewer viruses survive. My work gets even more detailed when I add factors like Scrubbing Friction or temperature, but the basic idea is the same: use chemistry to pop that virus like a balloon!
think_abouts: Why do you think the fat-loving end of a soap molecule is the key to destroying a virus's membrane?
Dr. Al-Rashid models how her surfaces work. How is that similar to the model you are building in class?

vocabulary.definition: A molecule of fat or oil. Viruses often have an outer coating made of lipids.
vocabulary.use_sentence: The soap molecule attached to the lipid layer of the virus.

vocabulary.definition: A thin, protective outer layer that surrounds a cell or a virus.
vocabulary.use_sentence: Once the virus's membrane was broken, it could no longer cause harm.

vocabulary.definition: Describes a molecule that is attracted to both water and fats/oils, like soap.
vocabulary.use_sentence: The amphiphilic nature of soap allows it to wash away both dirt and germs.

vocabulary.definition: A bacterium, virus, or other microorganism that can cause disease.
vocabulary.use_sentence: Washing your hands is a great way to remove a harmful pathogen.

discussion_cards.prompt: Dr. Al-Rashid designs germ-fighting surfaces for hospitals and schools. Where else would a self-cleaning surface be very useful? Why?
The story mentions that factors like 'Scrubbing Friction' can be added to the model. What other factors do you think could affect how well soap works?

organizer.title: Cause and Effect: How Soap Works

boxes.label: Cause
boxes.content: More soap is used (Higher Soap Concentration)

boxes.label: Effect
boxes.content: The virus's lipid membrane is broken apart

boxes.label: Cause
boxes.content: The virus's lipid membrane is broken

reflection_frames.knew: Before reading, I already knew that ___
reflection_frames.learned: From the story, I learned that ___
